title: The Veil of Abyssal Light
blurb: A prince swims through the wreckage of his shattered wedding—crown crushed, memory lost, only the abyss to guide him. Every shadow whispers treason. Every glance could be betrayal. The throne he’s never known waits beneath a tide of lies.
comparable: For fans of *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch, *The Name of the Wind* by Patrick Rothfuss, and *The Priory of the Orange Tree* by Samantha Shannon

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The lower archives of the Lumina Spire smelled of salt and old paper. Kaelan Veyne adjusted the brass lens over his left eye, angling the dim bioluminescent light to catch the fine veins of the algae sample pinned beneath the microscope. The glow shifted from emerald to sapphire as he rotated the dial, tracing the strain’s morphology with the tip of his pen.

“You’re doing it again,” said a nasal voice from behind him.

Kaelan didn’t look up. “Doing what?”

Taelis sidled up to the workbench, clutching a dog-eared lexicon. “That exacting precision. Like you’re afraid the algae might correct you if you blink wrong.”

Kaelan capped his pen. “Some of us take taxonomy seriously.”

“Seris takes it seriously. You take it like a man marking graves.” Taelis flipped open his book. “You’re supposed to be a concubine, Veyne. Not a funeral director.”

The student’s laughter drew a dry click from the librarian’s desk.

Librarian Vexis straightened in her creaking chair, fingers steepled over a ledger. “Enough. Both of you. The archives aren’t a tavern.”

Kaelan turned back to his notes, but the words blurred. His left wrist burned beneath the rolled linen cuff. The sigil—faint as starlight—had flickered when he opened the box this morning. He thumbed the edge of the sample slide, pretending to recalibrate the light.

Taelis leaned closer. “You’re bleeding.”

Kaelan froze. A single bead of crimson welled on his wrist. He pressed his sleeve down, smiling. “Old habit. Ink.”

“Since when?”

“Since always.”

Vexis exhaled through her nose, the sound like a tide withdrawing. “Taelis, the lumen samples. Now.”

The student shuffled away, muttering about obsessions and royal bloodlines.

Kaelan exhaled. The bead vanished into the linen fibers. He rotated the microscope slightly, letting the beam of light cut across the wall behind him. The algae strains shimmered in concentric rings, a living clock marking time he didn’t have.

A shadow fell across the table.

Kaelan didn’t need to look up to know it wasn’t Taelis or Vexis. The air thickened; the algae’s glow dimmed as if shaded by intention.

“You’re late.”

Captain Elias Torin stood rigid in his Dominion Guard coat, the epaulets catching the dim light like twin moons. His jawline was sharper than Kaelan remembered, the scar above his eyebrow more pronounced in the low glow.

Kaelan tapped the pen against the bench. “I wasn’t aware we had an appointment.”

Elias didn’t sit. “The Chancellor wants the Maris algae catalog cross-referenced with the Dominion ledgers. By tonight.”

Kaelan capped the inkwell. “I’m sure your calculators can manage.”

Elias’s gaze flicked to the ink-stained fingers, then to the rolled sleeve. “Your wrist.”

Kaelan folded his hands. “An old cut.”

Elias didn’t blink. “I inspect injuries.”

Kaelan almost laughed. Almost. Instead, he rolled the sleeve higher. The sigil pulsed once, faint as a fish in the deep.

Elias’s eyes narrowed. The scar on his brow twitched.

Kaelan dropped the sleeve. “Satisfied?”

Elias exhaled through his nose. “The Chancellor expects the catalog on his desk by dusk. Don’t disappoint him again.”

He turned to leave.

Kaelan watched him go. Elias’s stride was measured, disciplined—but there was a stiffness in his shoulders Kaelan hadn’t noticed before. Like a man carrying something he hadn’t asked for.

The algae on the bench dimmed further, as if sensing the tension. Kaelan’s pen hovered over the ledger. His fingers trembled.

He forced his hand steady.

Below the inkwell, just visible in the corner of his vision, a seam in the desk’s wood panel gaped slightly. Kaelan had noticed it weeks ago. He had touched it once, when Vexis was absent. The panel had shifted imperceptibly.

He reached now, brushing his thumb along the crack.

The wood gave. A hollow cavity yawned open.

Inside lay a single sheet of vellum, rolled tightly. Kaelan’s breath caught. The royal cipher—hidden for fifteen years—glowed faintly against his palm.

Only he could see it.

Chapter 2

The Grand Atrium shimmered. Bioluminescent panels lined the domed ceiling in slow waves of indigo and gold, casting the assembled crowd in flickering tidepool light. Kaelan adjusted the high collar of his borrowed robe—dark blue, unadorned—and kept his hands clasped behind his back. The crowd parted for him, not with deference, but with the instinctive recoil of those who recognized the scent of failure.

Seris stood at the lectern, holding Kaelan’s manuscript in both hands. The pages trembled. Not from nerves, he knew. From calculation. She had practiced this look: daughter of a scribe, trained to dissect words as others did flesh.

Chancellor Dravik Solmarn sat in the first row, motionless as a deep-sea anemone. His niece, Lady Neria, draped in pearlescent silk, fanned herself delicately. Her gaze flicked between Seris and Kaelan like a blade between ribs.

Kaelan watched the inkwell at Seris’s elbow.

It was the one he had used for years. The one with the false bottom. The one that now held the vial of royal blood he had drawn after the death of his foster father, the marine biologist who had raised him. The vial that carried the proof he was who he claimed not to be.

Seris cleared her throat. The acoustics of the Atrium carried her voice upward, toward the gilded frieze of the Maris Dynasty.

“I come before you not as a scribe, but as a servant of the Dominion,” she said.

A ripple passed through the crowd. Kaelan let his fingers brush the cuff of his sleeve. The sigil beneath the linen flared once, weak as dying embers.

Seris continued. “In the interest of transparency, I must address certain irregularities in the work of Veyne the Scholar.”

Neria Solmarn stopped fanning herself.

Kaelan kept his face smooth. He had read every footnote in that manuscript. He knew exactly what Seris was about to say.

She opened the volume. “The final chapter of *The Bioluminescence of the Abyss* contains obscure citations—footnotes referencing Dominion protocols that were not publicly available when the manuscript was submitted for review.”

Dravik Solmarn’s fingers twitched against the armrest of his chair.

Seris turned a page. “Moreover, these citations match passages from classified Solmarn ledgers—not once, but repeatedly. Too precisely to be coincidence.”

A gasp. A cough. The Atrium hummed.

Kaelan’s wrist burned. The sigil pulsed against his skin, hot and urgent. Only he could see it.

Seris lifted the inkwell. “Even the ink used by Veyne stains differently. It carries traces of a solvent used only in the archival vaults.”

She tilted the well. A droplet fell onto the lectern. It gleamed like captured lightning.

Dravik stood.

The crowd instinctively recoiled further. He was a tall man, narrow-faced, his voice low and precise.

“Seris Veyra,” he said. “Are you accusing Prince Kaelan Veyne of plagiarism?”

Seris hesitated. One heartbeat. Two.

Then she spoke.

“Yes.”

The word hung in the bioluminescent air.

Kaelan felt the weight of the crowd’s gaze like pressure in his skull. He did not flinch. He did not speak.

Dravik stepped forward. “As Regent of the Dominion, I must act. This manuscript will be impounded. The author will face inquiry.”

Seris’s hands shook.

Kaelan finally moved. He stepped forward, his bare feet silent on the polished coral floor.

“Chancellor,” he said, “you misunderstand. The inkwell does not belong to me.”

Neria’s fan snapped shut.

Dravik’s eyes narrowed. “Explain.”

Kaelan reached out. Seris recoiled, but he took the inkwell anyway. He turned it slowly in his hands. The vial inside sloshed, invisible to all but him.

“This inkwell has been in the archive for decades,” he said. “Passed from scribe to scribe. Used in every royal census since the Tideborn Eclipse.”

A lie. But it would do.

Dravik’s gaze fixed on the well. “You expect me to believe the Commonwealth’s most trusted reagent vial was at your disposal?”

Kaelan met his eyes. “I expect you to test it.”

Silence.

Then Neria laughed—a sharp, brittle sound.

“Oh, Chancellor,” she said. “Let him. Let’s see what color the stains turn when spread on the truth.”

Dravik did not smile. “Very well.”

Kaelan uncorked the well. The scent of aged ink and royal solvent rose into the Atrium.

He dipped the bone stylus from his robe.

And brushed it across the first clean parchment on the lectern.

The ink did not spread. It *gathered*. A sigil formed on the page—not in pigment, but in radiant light. A complex knot of lines, glowing faintly violet.

The sigil of House Maris.

The crowd erupted.

Dravik’s face darkened. Neria dropped her fan. It clattered to the floor.

Kaelan held the stylus steady.

The proof was out.

And in the inkwell, the vial of royal blood swirled like a captured star.

Chapter 3

Kaelan’s study was a bubble of quiet beneath the Lumina Spire, carved into the living stone where bioluminescent algae grew sparse and dim. The room smelled of salt and old paper, of ink that had never quite dried. A single lamp cast a wan blue glow over the desk, illuminating parchment, bone styluses, and a half-empty inkwell.

Seris stood in the doorway, her scribe’s robes damp from the canal mist. She had not knocked. She rarely did anymore.

Kaelan kept his back to her. The sharpening stone whispered against the stylus in his hand. Steel on bone.

“I wrote it down,” she said. “Every word.”

He didn’t look up. “You had to.”

“It was poison. You know it was.”

He dipped the stylus into the inkwell. The liquid clung to the bone, slow and thick, like tar drawn from the deep.

Seris stepped closer. Her shadow fell across the desk.

“I should have burned it.”

Kaelan exhaled through his nose. “You’d be burning yourself.”

She flinched. Not at the words, but at the inkwell. At the way the light inside it pulsed faintly, as if breathing.

“You always said my footnotes were too precise,” he went on. “Too specific. Like I had access to records that didn’t exist.”

Seris’s fingers curled into fists. “You *do* have access. You always did.”

He turned the well slowly. The glow deepened, then waned. Like a heartbeat.

Kaelan uncorked it. A scent rose—metallic, ancient, like the blood of kings preserved in glass. He dipped the stylus again.

Seris’s voice cracked. “What are you doing?”

“Finishing what I started.”

He traced the symbol onto fresh parchment: a sigil, complex and tangled, the mark of House Maris. The ink didn’t darken the page. It *illuminated* it. A faint violet glow seeped from the lines, then faded, leaving only memory.

Seris’s breath hitched.

Kaelan set the stylus down. Rubbed his thumb over the parchment. The glow pulsed once, then died.

Silence.

Seris swallowed. “You knew. All this time, you knew.”

He recorked the well. The light inside went still.

“I suspected.”

“And you let me expose you?”

Kaelan looked at her now. Not with anger. Not with pity. Just weariness.

“I had to be seen as a fraud first. Otherwise, they’d have killed me before I reached the Vault.”

Seris’s hands trembled. “The Chancellor thinks you’re a thief. A liar. He’ll never—”

“He already knows,” Kaelan said. “He just doesn’t want to.”

She stepped back. Her heel caught the edge of a stack of ledgers. They toppled. Paper scattered.

Kaelan didn’t move to help.

Seris knelt anyway, gathering the pages. Her fingers shook.

Kaelan watched the inkwell. The light inside it had changed. It was brighter now. Hungry.

Seris froze.

The glow wasn’t just seeping out. It was *rising*. A faint mist curled from the well, like steam from a hot spring. It spiraled upward, coalescing into a flickering shape—a face? A crown?

Seris recoiled. “What in the abyss—?”

Kaelan didn’t answer. He reached for the well.

Seris slapped his hand away. “Don’t touch it!”

He pulled back. “It’s just light.”

“It’s *hungry*.” Her voice was a whisper. “You’ve been hiding it all this time. Using it. But it’s not ink. It’s blood. *Your* blood.”

The mist thickened. A shape formed in the air above the well—a silhouette, tall and regal, crowned in shadow. It didn’t move. It didn’t speak. But the parchment on the desk trembled as if in a current.

Kaelan’s breath came shallow.

Seris’s eyes were wide. Not with fear. With *recognition*.

“Who are you?” she asked.

Not in accusation. Not in anger.

In dawning terror.

The inkwell’s contents swirled like captured starlight. The mist above it pulsed once, twice—then dissolved.

Kaelan exhaled. His shoulders dropped.

Seris didn’t move. “You didn’t answer.”

He looked at her. Really looked at her.

“I don’t know anymore.”

Chapter 4

The Chancellor’s sanctum perched at the Spire’s highest tier, a glass-walled cage suspended over the abyss. The air smelled of ozone and aged cedar, the scent of power preserved in wood and stone. Below, the city’s bioluminescent grid pulsed like a slow, living vein—green and gold, the colors of the Dominion’s might.

Chancellor Dravik Solmarn stood before the panoramic window, his back to the door. The weight of his ceremonial dagger rested against his thigh, the hilt carved in the sigil of the Maris line—a detail he no longer thought about.

Orlan, his advisor, entered without announcement, boots clicking on the wet stone. He carried a data slate, its surface flickering with reports. The Chancellor did not turn.

“They’ve authenticated the manuscript,” Orlan said. “The cipher is real. A Maris cipher, buried in the footnotes.”

Dravik exhaled through his nose. “As expected. The thief thought himself clever.”

“He did. And so did the scribe, until she realized.”

“Seris Veyra.” Dravik tasted the name like stale wine. “A scribe. A nobody. She used her own inkwell to expose a fraud. How poetic.”

Orlan hesitated. “There’s more.”

Dravik finally turned. His robes whispered against the floor.

“Well?”

“Unverified sightings. In the Abyssal Vault. A figure glowing in the restricted wing.”

The Chancellor’s fingers twitched toward his dagger. “A figment. A trick of the algae.”

“Or a ghost.”

Dravik’s smile was thin. “Ghosts are for poets. The Maris line is dead. We saw to that.”

He stepped back from the window, his gaze drifting to the city below. The algae farms spread like a carpet of emerald fire, pulsing in rhythm with the Council’s will.

Orlan cleared his throat. “What of the scribe? She must be contained.”

“She will be. When the time is right.” Dravik’s voice was calm, deliberate. “We have the thief’s name. Kaelan Veyne. A concubine’s bastard, playing at scholar. Let the city laugh at him awhile longer. Let the Council believe justice has been served.”

Orlan bowed his head. “And the Vault reports?”

Dravik plucked the dagger from his belt. The blade caught the dim light, gleaming like a promise.

“Ignore them.”

---

The Dominion Guard barracks sat in the Spire’s lower levels, a maze of iron and glass where duty smelled of salt and steel. Captain Elias Torin stood beneath the archway of the armory, his gloves still damp from drills. He had not slept in two days.

A guard approached, clutching a sealed order. “Captain, direct from the Chancellor’s sanctum.”

Elias broke the wax seal. The parchment crackled in his grip. “Seris Veyra,” he read aloud. “Charges: forgery, defamation, theft of intellectual property.”

The guard shifted. “She’s to be brought in for interrogation.”

Elias exhaled through his teeth. He knew Seris. Not well. But she had once brought him a cup of bitter tea when he stood watch through the Trench of Echoes. She had not called him *hero*. She had called him *tired*.

He crumpled the order in his fist. “Have her brought to the holding cells. Not the interrogation chambers. Not yet.”

The guard hesitated. “Captain?”

“Do it.”

He turned away before the man could question him further. His boots carried him down the corridor, where the walls hummed with the faint pulse of the algae grid. He paused at the observation deck overlooking the Grand Atrium.

The atrium was empty now. The remnants of the wedding tapestry still hung from the ceiling, its threads frayed where the guests had torn it down. The air smelled of salt tears and old perfume.

Elias pressed his palm against the glass. The surface was cool, indifferent.

A voice spoke behind him.

“You’re brooding.”

He did not turn. “I’m working.”

Captain Neria Solmarn stepped beside him, her uniform immaculate, her gloves pristine. She was the Chancellor’s niece. She was also the woman he had been ordered to arrest.

She tilted her head. “Seris Veyra? Really?”

“She exposed a fraud.”

Neria’s laugh was sharp. “A fraud you once inspected yourself. A concubine with a scholar’s hands. What kind of thief bleeds violet, captain?”

Elias clenched his jaw. He had seen the sigil. He had written the report. He had ignored it.

Neria’s voice dropped. “You know as well as I do that Dravik doesn’t tolerate mistakes. Especially not ones that implicate his niece in a lie.”

“You were jilted,” Elias said. “Not murdered.”

She met his gaze. “And you were ordered to arrest a nobody. Not a prince.”

The words struck like a blade between the ribs.

Elias turned fully now. “Careful, captain.”

Neria smiled. “I’m not afraid of you.”

“You should be.”

She stepped closer. Her hand brushed his arm, not a touch, but a claim. “You could walk away. Tonight. Leave the Spire. Leave the Dominion. You’re not a guard. You’re a soldier. A leader. You don’t have to do this.”

Elias pulled his arm back. “I have a duty.”

“Duty to who?” she asked. “A chancellor who lies? A Council that fears the light?”

He did not answer.

Neria’s smile faded. “You’ll regret it.”

She walked away, her boots ringing against the stone. Elias watched her go. Then he looked down at his hands.

They were trembling.

---

The Spire’s upper gardens were a gilded cage of glass and vine, where the elite of the Dominion strolled beneath a canopy of bioluminescent blossoms. Chancellor Dravik stood at the center of the path, his fingers drumming against his thigh.

Orlan approached, his face pale. “Chancellor. The Council has arrived.”

Dravik did not look at him. “And the guest of honor?”

“Kaelan Veyne has not been brought in yet.”

“He will be.” Dravik’s voice was calm, but his fingers tightened around the dagger. “He has no choice.”

Orlan hesitated. “And the Vault sightings?”

The Chancellor’s shoulders tensed. “They mean nothing.”

A gust of current rippled through the gardens, making the blossoms sway. For a moment, the light dimmed—just a flicker, as if something had passed between the city’s veins.

Dravik’s fingers twitched.

Then the light returned.

He exhaled. Smoothed his robes.

“Prepare the announcement,” he said. “Tonight, we will show the Dominion unity. We will show them strength.”

Orlan bowed. “And the prisoner?”

Dravik looked toward the Spire’s lower levels, where the holding cells waited.

“Bring him,” he said. “Bring him up to the atrium.”

The Chancellor turned back to the window. The city pulsed below him, green and gold and alive.

He did not see the faint violet light that flickered in the eastern quadrant of the algae grid.

He did not see the shadow that moved through the Vault’s restricted wing.

He only saw the future he had built.

And it was perfect.

Chapter 5

The Grand Atrium shimmered. Three hundred guests filled the chamber, their bioluminescent jewelry casting soft emerald halos on marble that remembered being marble once, before the city claimed it. Chandeliers of pressurized glass held captive the glow of deep-sea algae, pulsing in slow, synchronized waves like the breath of a sleeping god.

Chancellor Dravik Solmarn ascended the dais. His ceremonial robes dragged behind him, each step deliberate, each breath measured. He raised his arms. The crowd stilled.

“Citizens of the Abyssal Dominion,” Dravik began, his voice resonant, cultivated over years of broadcasting calm, “we stand tonight beneath the light that binds us—united, prosperous, unbroken.”

A ripple moved through the canopy of algae overhead. Not the measured pulse of the city’s rhythm—something stuttered. A flicker. A hesitation.

Dravik did not pause. “I have just returned from the Abyssal Vault, where I inspected the Dominion’s crown jewel: the algae-distribution reform. A system rebuilt from ruin to serve every district equally.”

Lady Orvanna, a Council Member from the northern trench, tilted her head backward. Her necklace pulsed in time with the grid, but its rhythm lagged.

“Chancellor,” she said, interrupting. “There’s a—”

The algae grid dimmed. A single quadrant above the dais—east sector, where the archives once stood—withered from emerald to sickly gray. The canopy stuttered. A cough in the city’s lungs.

Guests inhaled. A woman dropped her glass. It shattered on the stone, the sound swallowed by the sudden hush.

Dravik’s voice did not waver. “A localized fluctuation. Temporary. Our systems are resilient.”

The dim spread. Not a failure. A retreat. The algae shrank like a tide withdrawing, exposing the dark stone of the Spire’s ceiling. The crowd gasped.

Then—silence.

The eastern quadrant flickered back to life. But not fully. It flickered. Pulsed. Like a dying heart.

Dravik’s fingers tightened around the podium. His knuckles whitened. He continued speaking, but his voice cracked on the word *unbroken*.

Orvanna pointed upward. “That’s where the Maris archives were.”

The words fell like a stone into still water.

Dravik did not look up. His goblet was already in hand, lifted from a nearby tray by a servant who did not meet his eyes. He drank deeply. Wine and power and the taste of control slipping.

His hand shook.

The chandelier above flickered again—once, twice—before steadying. The crowd exhaled. Dravik wiped his palm on his robe, the damp fabric clinging to his skin.

Across the atrium, Captain Elias Torin stood rigid, one hand resting on the hilt of his service blade. His duty was to protect the Chancellor, to maintain order, to believe in the system that trained him. His knuckles were white.

Beside him, Neria Solmarn watched the Chancellor with eyes that missed nothing. She held a glass of chilled amber, the liquid untouched. The light caught the silver threading in her sleeves, the Solmarn sigil woven into the fabric—two serpents swallowing their tails, unbroken, eternal.

“You’re staring,” she said, not looking at Torin.

“I’m watching.”

“He’ll call it a fluctuation. Again.” She took a slow sip. “He’s good at that.”

Torin’s jaw flexed. “You believe it’s sabotage?”

Neria smiled. “Does it matter what I believe?”

He turned slightly toward her. “You know what I saw. The sigil. The blood. A Maris cipher smuggled into a footnote.”

“And yet the Chancellor still breathes.” She set her glass down. Untouched. “He breathes because the Council believes. Not because he tells the truth.”

A ripple of applause swelled through the atrium. Dravik had finished his speech. He lifted his goblet in a toast. The crowd responded, their bioluminescence pulsing brighter in rhythm, obeying the command of performance.

Torin did not raise his glass.

Neria watched him. “You’re thinking of walking away.”

“Walking away isn’t an option.”

“It is for some.”

“Not for a soldier.”

“Soldiers choose which wars to fight.”

Torin’s gaze locked onto the Chancellor, who was now circulating through the crowd, handshakes and smiles, a man who had just survived a tremor in the grid.

“He’s hiding something,” Torin said.

Neria’s voice dropped. “You think the Maris line is alive?”

“I think the archives flickered where the Maris archives once stood.”

She exhaled, amused. “Or the algae is sick. Or the Council has finally pushed too far.”

Torin said nothing.

Neria stepped closer. Her perfume mingled with the salt and ozone of the atrium. “You could arrest me tonight. Serve the Chancellor’s will. Or you could walk into the shadows with me.”

“And do what?”

“Survive what’s coming.” She met his gaze. “The eclipse isn’t just a darkening of the algae. It’s the Dominion’s reckoning. And the Maris bloodline won’t stay buried forever.”

Torin felt the weight of his blade, the weight of his oath. “You’re asking me to betray my duty.”

Neria’s eyes gleamed. “I’m asking you to be alive when the light returns.”

The atrium throbbed with music, with laughter, with the illusion of unity. The Chancellor raised his goblet high. The crowd mirrored him.

Torin did not raise his.

His hand hovered over his blade. Not to draw. Not yet.

Neria leaned in, her voice a whisper. “Tonight, the Vault Keeper will report a shadow. Tomorrow, it will be a ghost. And by the eclipse, it will be a king.”

She turned away, her uniform crisp, her words a blade unsharpened but certain.

Torin watched her go. Then he looked down at his hands.

They were steady.

The atrium’s canopy pulsed. The western quadrant dimmed. Not from the east this time.

From the south.

A second fluctuation.

Dravik’s goblet trembled.

He looked up.

And saw the grid stutter again.

This time, it did not recover.

Chapter 6

The Tidal Passage smelled of iron and old wine. Kaelan moved through the dark, his fingers tracing the wall’s rough surface, counting paces—twenty-three, twenty-four—until the stone gave way to slick coral, then open water. The current here was gentle, a lazy drift from the deep trenches, carrying the scent of sediment and the faintest echo of distant algae farms. He exhaled, watching the bubbles rise, small silver things against the black.

No light. Not his.

He wore the borrowed uniform of a Vault Keeper’s assistant: stiff black fabric, the hem frayed, the collar too tight across his throat. The insignia was sewn on crooked, but that only helped. In the Dominion, mediocrity was invisible.

His sigil pulsed against his wrist, faint but insistent, like the afterimage of a flash. Five tiny arcs of light, arranged like a star. Only royal archives recognized it. Only his blood could make it glow.

He slipped through the final arch, into the Abyssal Vault.

The air here was cooler, mineral-scented, thick with the weight of centuries. Rows of glass domes stretched into the dark, each holding a fragment of the Dominion’s memory: scrolls sealed in resin, tablets inscribed with the first laws of the Spire, fragments of coral inscribed with the names of dead Councilors. The centerpiece—a massive central desk of black basalt—sat empty except for a single pedestal, its stone worn smooth by centuries of scholars’ hands.

Kaelan dropped to one knee. From his belt, he drew a small vial, its glass stoppered with wax. Inside, a single drop of dried blood, rust-colored, almost black in the dim. He uncorked it with his teeth and let the drop fall into the inkwell at the center of the desk. The liquid hissed, then settled.

A cipher bloomed across the inkwell’s surface, etched in gold—a pattern of loops and whorls, the royal cipher of House Maris. It shimmered once, then faded, absorbed.

He stood, wiping his palms on his thighs.

Now.

He reached into the inner pocket of his coat and pulled out a single scale. Not any scale—an artifact from the Rise of Maris, salvaged by his adoptive father decades ago. The fish that wore it had died out a century before the coup. Its iridescence was legendary: deep violet at the edges, bleeding to crimson at the center, like captured sunset.

He pressed it into the center of the desk.

The scale adhered instantly, its edges curling slightly as if tasting the stone. Then it pulsed. Once. Twice. A heartbeat in the dark.

Kaelan stepped back. The Vault’s bioluminescent sconces flared weakly—an automatic response to disturbance, a system designed to alert Keeper’s to intrusion. But the pulse of the scale absorbed the light, drawing it inward, channeling it into a slow, rhythmic glow. Like a signal.

Like a claim.

He turned to leave, retracing his steps through the archives, past the domes of silent history. At the far arch, he paused. One last look.

The scale’s light still pulsed.

Good.

The Vault Keeper would find it by dawn. And by dawn, the Chancellor would know the archives were no longer silent.

Kaelan slipped into the passage just as the sconces dimmed again, as if satisfied. The current tugged at him, pulling him deeper into the dark. He kicked once, twice, and vanished beneath the weight of the city.

---

Dawn came in layers.

First, the algae farms flickered to life, their usual emerald rhythm returning—steady, predictable. Then the servants stirred in the Spire’s upper tiers, lighting braziers, laying out breakfast trays of dried squid and kelp cakes. Finally, the Vault Keeper arrived, a gaunt figure in silver robes, his gills fluttering with nervous exertion.

He did not expect a scale.

Not here. Not now.

He entered the archives through the main hall, his steps echoing. The sconces flared, obedient. He moved to the central desk, adjusted a scroll, then froze.

Something glowed.

A single violet point of light, resting on the basalt like a jewel dropped by a careless god. It pulsed, slow and regular, like a heartbeat.

The Keeper dropped to his knees.

He reached out. His fingers hovered. He had cataloged every artifact in this vault. He knew its history, its secrets. This scale was not on any ledger. Not in any archive.

He touched it.

The light flared, bright enough to cast shadow. The Keeper recoiled, gasping. His breath came in short bursts, stirring the fine silt at the base of the desk.

He looked up.

The archives were breathing.

Not the algae. Not the sconces.

The archives themselves.

The light from the scale spread, creeping along the desk’s edge, up the pedestal, across the floor—thin threads of violet and crimson, weaving into the cracks, the seams, the ancient mortar. It pooled in the air, hovering like mist, then sank into the stone as if the Vault were inhaling.

The Keeper’s fingers trembled. He fumbled for his comm bead, pressed it.

“Report,” came the voice of the Chancellor’s aide. “Vault status?”

The Keeper’s mouth opened. Closed.

“Archives…” he stammered. “The archives have begun to… breathe.”

Silence on the other end. Then, sharply: “Repeat that.”

“The archives—”

A distant alarm wailed.

The comm cut.

The Keeper stared at the scale. It pulsed again.

Then it went dark.

But the breath remained.

Chapter 7

The Grand Atrium of the Lumina Spire was never meant to hold this many people. The domed ceiling arched high above, its bioluminescent veins pulsing in time with the Council’s heartbeat—slow, deliberate, unshaken. But today, the light stuttered. Once. A flicker no one else caught. A glitch in the system. Or something worse.

Kaelan stood at the back of the chamber, pressed between two Council aides. Their robes smelled of salt and old parchment. He kept his head down, his fingers tucked into the sleeves of his stolen scribe’s coat—Seris’s coat, actually, worn thin at the elbows. The hem dragged in the algae-grown carpet, leaving faint gray streaks. He didn’t dare lift it.

Chancellor Dravik Solmarn sat upon the Obsidian Seat, his back straight as a knife’s edge. The throne was carved from a single slab of volcanic glass, black and reflective, no light escaping its surface. He wore the ceremonial robes of office today: deep crimson, embroidered with silver eels. His gills flared slightly as he inhaled, the only sign of life beneath the stillness.

“Let us proceed,” Dravik said. His voice carried, low and layered by the chamber’s acoustics. “The charges against the pretender Kaelan Veyne have been substantiated. His manuscript—”

Seris’s manuscript—now branded as his.

“—contains stolen intellectual property, falsified credentials, and a deliberate attempt to undermine the Dominion’s scholarly integrity.”

A ripple of agreement moved through the Council. Heads nodded. A few scribes shuffled papers. The air smelled of ink and nervous sweat.

Kaelan exhaled through his nose. Beside him, a junior clerk dropped a stylus. It clattered against the marble floor, the sound too loud. He scrambled to pick it up, his cheeks flushed.

Dravik’s gaze tightened. Not at the noise. At the empty seat to his right.

The seat of the Heir of Maris.

Always vacant since the coup.

Now it felt heavier.

Nereia Solmarn stood. She was Dravik’s niece, but not his daughter. Not by blood. Her gown was the color of deep coral, woven with threads that shimmered like trapped sunlight. She moved with the precision of someone who had practiced this moment in mirrors for years.

“Honorable Council,” she said, voice steady, “I must ask—do we not mourn the absence of a legitimate heir?”

A hush fell. The scribe froze mid-sentence.

Dravik’s fingers curled into the armrests. “Nereia, you speak of ghosts.”

“Do I?” Her eyes flickered to the chair. “Fifteen years is a long mourning.”

“Or a convenient story.” Dravik’s voice was calm, but beneath it, something brittle. “My brother and his wife died in a diving accident. Their child with them. There are records. Official decrees.”

“Records can be forged,” she said.

Dravik’s lips parted—

A scroll unfurled across the chamber floor.

It had been dropped. Again. But this time, it wasn’t a stylus. This time, the parchment was old, brittle, the edges torn as if pulled from a hidden seam. The ink was faded, but legible: *Maris–Veyne Contract of Binding, Year of the Tidefall.*

A marriage contract.

Signed.

In blood.

The scroll settled at Dravik’s feet, unrolling fully. The Council leaned in. A few gasped. A historian gasped loudly.

Kaelan’s pulse hammered in his throat. He recognized the signature. Not his own—his parents. Prince Maris and Princess Veyne. His blood.

Dravik didn’t move. Didn’t breathe. His face went from bronze to gray in three heartbeats.

Nereia’s eyes burned into the parchment. “This is *your* handwriting, uncle. On *your* marriage decree.”

Dravik’s voice cracked like ice under pressure. “That is—”

“A lie?” she finished. “Or a secret you’ve kept fifteen years?”

Silence.

Then the chamber doors burst open.

Captain Elias Torin stood there, clad in the black and silver of the Dominion Guard. His uniform was immaculate. His jaw was set. His eyes—

They locked onto Kaelan for half a second too long.

Then shifted away.

“My apologies,” Elias said, stepping forward. “The session is interrupted.”

Dravik didn’t look at him. He stared at the scroll, his breath shallow.

“By what right?” Nereia demanded.

Elias held up a sealed order. “By the Chancellor’s own decree. The archives have reported an anomaly. The Vault is—”

A distant chime echoed through the Spire. Not the usual harmonic. Not the algae pulse. A deep, resonant tone, like a bell tolling in the dark.

The Council turned.

The chime came again.

From the west wing.

From the Vault.

Dravik’s knuckles whitened.

The chime wasn’t mechanical.

It was organic.

The algae grid flickered—once, twice—across the grand atrium’s domed ceiling. The bioluminescence dimmed. Not like a storm. Like a breath held too long.

Then it flickered back.

But the damage was done.

Nereia’s voice cut through the haze. “The Vault is crying out. And it’s calling *him*.”

Dravik stood abruptly. The Obsidian Seat groaned.

Kaelan felt the weight of every eye in the room.

Even Elias’s.

Then the chamber lights dimmed for the third time.

And in that flicker of dark, Kaelan saw it:

Dravik’s face.

Not angry.

Terrified.

The chime sounded again.

Deeper.

Closer.

The Grand Atrium held its breath—and waited.

Chapter 8

The Trench of Echoes did not welcome visitors.

Dravik Solmarn knew this. His boots pressed carefully into the silt, each step deliberate, testing the crumbling ledge. His guard flanked him, armored in dull bronze, their gleaming tridents casting wavering shadows on the trench wall. The bioluminescence here was weak—patchy. Like dying stars fading behind deep-sea mist.

“It’s not safe, Chancellor,” Advisor Orlan muttered, his voice crackling through the internal comm. His gills fluttered anxiously. “The algae won’t stabilize. The readings are erratic.”

Dravik didn’t answer. He didn’t need to. The air smelled wrong. Not just the metallic tang of the trench, but something older. Something beneath the stone.

They reached the communications outpost—a rusted bronze dome embedded in the wall, its porthole fogged with condensation. Inside, two guards stood over a distressed marine technician.

“Sir,” the man gasped, “it started two hours ago. First just the memorials. Then the statues of the old kings. We thought it was a power surge, but—”

“Show me,” Dravik said.

The technician tapped a worn console. The dome’s outer light dimmed. On the screen, a feed from the Rise of Maris flickered to life.

The statues.

Twelve stone effigies of the Maris line, lined along the palace’s grand colonnade. They had stood silent for fifteen years. No algae glowed on their surfaces. No light lived in their carved eyes.

Until now.

One by one, the statues began to pulse. Soft blue-green light spread across their surfaces, like ink spreading in water. The oldest, the first Maris, glowed first—a faint shimmer in the stone. Then the next. Then the next, like a wave moving up the line.

Dravik’s stomach twisted.

“It’s not system-generated,” the technician said. “The algae nodes are offline. These are reacting *independently*. Like… like they’re remembering.”

Dravik turned away. “Cut the feed.”

The screen went dark.

Orlan exhaled sharply. “Chancellor, if word spreads—”

“It won’t,” Dravik snapped. “Because we control the grid. We control the narrative.”

The guards exchanged glances.

Dravik ignored them. He stepped closer to the trench wall, where a narrow ledge led downward toward the abyss. The water beyond was black, impenetrable.

“There,” he said. “The whispers started there. At the base. Like voices speaking through the current.”

A shiver ran down Orlan’s spine. He didn’t look. “You can’t go down there.”

Dravik didn’t answer. He removed his ceremonial sash, leaving only his robe. He crouched, then stepped onto the ledge. His weight shifted the stone. A pebble tumbled into the dark.

No splash.

No echo.

Just silence.

Then—a sound.

A whisper.

Not through the comm. Not in the water.

From the trench itself.

Like a voice shaped by moving water.

Dravik froze.

The guards shifted uncomfortably. One gripped his trident harder.

“Chancellor?” Orlan’s voice was tight.

Dravik didn’t move. He listened.

Then he stood.

“Return to the Spire,” he said. “Tell no one of this.”

“But the algae—”

“The algae is reacting,” Dravik said. “But not to power. To *memory*.”

Orlan paled. “Memory?”

Dravik turned. His eyes were cold. “The Maris bloodline is not extinct. It is awake.”

Orlan opened his mouth to protest, but Dravik was already stepping off the ledge. For a heartbeat, he hung in the water. Then he descended, robe billowing like a funeral shroud, into the abyss.

The guards didn’t follow.

They couldn’t.

The trench had claimed men before.

And tonight, it wanted more.

The Dominion Guard barracks smelled of brine and polished steel. Captain Elias Torin stood in the center of the armory, running a whetstone along the edge of his dagger. The blade gleamed under the algae lanterns, its reflection sharp enough to cut the air.

The door slid open.

Seris Veyra entered without knocking. Her coat was damp at the hem, her hair tangled from the tide. She held a sealed message tube in one hand.

Elias didn’t look up. “You’re not on duty.”

“I don’t need to be,” she said. “Because you do.”

He set the dagger down. “Speak plainly.”

She tossed the tube onto the table between them. It skidded, leaving a wet ring on the metal surface.

Elias picked it up. The wax seal was broken. The parchment inside carried the Dominion crest—freshly pressed.

Seris leaned in. “That’s from the Spire Council ledger. A merchant’s smuggling route. It was slipped into the inkwell exchange roster.”

Elias unfolded it.

*To the lost prince: Return at once. The algae bleeds. The Vault remembers. You are needed.*

No signature.

No sender.

But Elias knew the handwriting.

Old. Formal. Royal.

He crumpled the parchment in his fist.

“He’s using our own systems,” Seris said. “The ledgers. The archives. The courier routes. He’s rebuilding the Maris court in the dark.”

Elias didn’t answer. He stared at the crumpled paper, the ink bleeding into the fibers.

“You were ordered to arrest him,” Seris pressed. “You still can.”

“And take him where?” Elias snapped. “To Dravik? To a trial that’s already been decided?”

“To justice,” she said.

He looked up. His eyes were hard. “What justice? The one where they throw him into the Trench? The one where they let the algae eat itself into collapse? Or the one where they rewrite history so cleanly no one remembers the blood?”

Seris’s breath hitched. She hadn’t expected that.

Elias stood. He pinned the crumpled message to the table with his dagger. Then he walked to the weapons rack and took down his trident.

“Where are you going?” she asked.

“To see a man who’s more prince than I am a captain,” he said. “And to find out what the hell he’s doing to my city.”

Seris stepped forward. “Elias. Wait. He’s dangerous. He’s—”

“Awake,” Elias said. “And so are you.”

She fell silent.

He walked past her, toward the barracks exit, the trident balanced in his hand.

She called after him.

“Elias!”

He paused, but didn’t turn.

“What if he’s not the heir?” she said. “What if he’s just a ghost with a knife?”

Elias didn’t answer.

He stepped into the corridor.

The algae lanterns flickered as he passed.

The city was bleeding.

And so was he.

Chapter 9

The lower stacks of the Royal Scriptoria smelled of damp vellum and iron gall ink. Seris Veyra stood in the narrow aisle between shelves, spine straight, fingers brushing the spines of codices. The ledger lay open on the reading desk, its calfskin pages splayed like broken wings.

Kaelan didn’t look up from his desk. He dipped his pen into the inkwell, let a single drop fall onto the parchment. The black spread in a lazy bloom.

Seris closed the distance in two strides. “You saw it.”

He didn’t acknowledge her. Instead, he drew a wavy line across the page, shaping a tide mark—the cipher for *return*.

“They’re offering you mercy,” she said.

Still no response. He signed the bottom of the page with a flourish, then blew gently over the ink. It dried in an instant.

Seris snatched the page before he could roll it. “Dravik wants you back. The algae is bleeding in the Rise of Maris. The statues are glowing. The Council is panicking.”

Kaelan set the ledger aside. His fingers hovered over the embers of a low brazier, warming the last of the royal vellum he had bought under a false name. The paper curled slightly at the edges.

“And you brought this to me,” he said, “because?”

“Because you look like you’ve already decided to go,” she said. “And you shouldn’t. Not yet.”

He reached out. Not for the ledger. For the inkwell.

Seris pulled back. “Kaelan, listen to me—”

He plunged his fingers into the ink. Black dripped onto the cipher.

Then he dipped the parchment into the well.

The page hissed. Smoke curled upward. The cipher burned in reverse, the ink melting away in eerie symmetry, as though the fire moved inward instead of out.

Seris recoiled. “What are you doing?”

He watched, calm, as the parchment dissolved into wet ash. The scent of charred algae filled the air—sharp, ancient.

“He thinks he’s offering me a place at the table,” Kaelan said. “He’s offering me a trick.”

“Then don’t go,” she said.

He leaned back in his chair. The brazier’s glow caught the sigil on his wrist—the Maris mark, pulsing faintly, as if asleep.

“If I don’t go,” he said, “the algae dies. The statues darken. The Council meets in emergency session. And Dravik gets to rewrite history without witnesses.”

Seris exhaled sharply. “You’re going.”

“Not on his terms,” Kaelan said.

He stood. Shook the ash from his fingers.

Seris blocked the door. “You can’t just walk into the Spire. Not after what I published. Not after the arrest warrant.”

Kaelan stepped closer. Close enough that she could see the faintest blue-green glow beneath his skin, threading through his veins like tide lines in marble.

“Tell him,” Kaelan said softly, “that I’ll come when the tide turns.”

She didn’t move. “That’s not an answer.”

He smiled. Not the careful scholar’s smile. The prince’s.

“The only answer he’ll get,” he said.

Then he reached past her and opened the door.

Cold sea air rushed in, carrying the distant hum of the Dominion Grid.

Seris stayed where she was, rooted in the scriptorium’s quiet.

Kaelan paused on the threshold. “Seris.”

“Yes?”

“Burn the ledger.”

He stepped into the corridor.

The door swung shut behind him.

The ashes in the brazier spun upward in a slow spiral, defying gravity, defying reason.

And from the shadows of the stack aisle, a single drop of ink fell from the ledger’s spine, pooling black on the floor.

It didn’t spread.

It *waited*.

Chapter 10

The Rise of Maris loomed through the dark water, its skeletal towers clawing upward like ribs of some long-dead leviathan. The moon—what passed for a moon beneath the waves—cast a dim, sickly green light through the algae-laden currents, turning the ruins into a graveyard of light.

Kaelan kicked once, slow and deliberate, letting the current pull him toward the shattered throne. His wrist burned. The sigil pulsed like a second heartbeat, its bioluminescence threading up his arm in delicate, glowing veins. He flexed his fingers, watching as the pattern spread across his chest, mapping the Maris bloodline in living ink.

He hadn’t come to weep over broken marble. Not tonight.

The gardens around the throne were overgrown with dead coral and brittle kelp, the once-vibrant blooms now dull and gray. But the algae in the soil didn’t match the rot. Tiny flecks of light flickered in the silt, refusing to die.

Kaelan reached the base of the dais. The throne itself was a jagged ruin, split down the center, its royal sigil worn away by time and water. He placed his palm against the cold stone and pressed.

Nothing.

He exhaled through his nose. Closed his eyes.

Then he aligned his body with the carvings.

His wrist met the broken armrest. The sigil flared. His skin burned. The bioluminescent threads flared outward, tracing the ancient pattern etched into the throne—lines of power, veins of authority, the full map of the Maris Dynasty.

His breath hitched.

The carvings lit up.

Not with static light—with motion. The bioluminescence surged like blood through living tissue, pulsing in time with his heartbeat. The veins spread across the throne, up the shattered pillars, through the ruins of the palace. The gardens ignited in a spiral, a map leading inward, downward, toward the heart of the Dominion.

Kaelan stepped back. The sigil on his wrist blazed, searing hot. He clenched his jaw against the pain, but didn’t look away.

A voice echoed through the water—ancient, resonant, layered with something not quite human.

*You have returned.*

Kaelan didn’t answer. Not yet.

He raised his hands. The light from his veins responded, weaving through the ruins like threads of silk in a current. The broken pillars trembled. Dust sifted from the ceilings.

The gardens ignited in a controlled spiral, a path of light cutting through the dark water toward the heart of the Spire. The algae responded—alive, intelligent, obedient now, as if waking from a long sleep.

Kaelan knew what this was.

A test.

A summons.

A command.

He took a step forward. Then another.

The ruins creaked. Stone shifted. The throne’s broken half groaned, tilting slightly, as if making way.

Kaelan didn’t hesitate. He climbed the dais.

The sigil on his wrist pulsed faster, brighter. His skin shimmered in the dim light, veins glowing blue-green, the Maris mark now fully awake.

He knelt before the throne.

And waited.

Not for Dravik.

Not for the Council.

For the light to answer.

The gardens pulsed in perfect rhythm, the spiral tightening. The path led to one place only: the Dominion Power Core.

The source of all authority in the Abyssal Dominion.

The source of Dravik’s power.

The source Kaelan was meant to reclaim.

A shadow moved in the corner of his vision.

Kaelan didn’t turn. He didn’t need to.

“You’re glowing,” said Elias Torin, stepping from the darkness.

Kaelan didn’t look up. “So are you.”

Elias wore the Dominion Guard uniform, its deep indigo scales dull in the dim light. His trident hung at his side, but his hand rested lightly on the hilt—not threatening, but not relaxed either.

“You’re standing in the ruins of a dead dynasty,” Elias said.

Kaelan finally lifted his gaze. The sigil on his wrist flared, casting his face in ghostly light. “No. The ruins of one that’s waking up.”

Elias stepped closer. The current shifted around him. “You destroyed the cipher.”

“Ink on paper,” Kaelan said. “Not proof. Not power.”

“Then what is this?” Elias gestured at the glowing ruins, the spiraling path, the throne pulsing beneath Kaelan’s knees.

Kaelan flexed his fingers. The sigil pulsed in response. “The truth.”

Elias exhaled sharply through his nose. “You’re not a scholar anymore.”

“No,” Kaelan said. “I’m not.”

A long silence. The ruins hummed, alive with light.

Then Elias spoke, voice low. “Dravik’s got a warrant out for your arrest.”

Kaelan almost smiled. “I know.”

“He’ll have the Guard lock the Spire down.”

“Let him.”

Elias’ jaw tightened. “You’re walking into a trap.”

“No,” Kaelan said. “I’m walking into a coronation.”

Elias didn’t flinch. Didn’t laugh. Didn’t look away.

But his hand twitched on the trident.

Kaelan stood slowly. The sigil throbbed, a steady pulse of light between them.

“You were ordered to bring me in,” Kaelan said. “So do it.”

Elias didn’t move.

The ruins pulsed again—the path of light flaring brighter, the gardens burning with renewed intensity.

Kaelan took a step forward. Their shoulders almost touched.

“Arrest me,” Kaelan whispered. “Or stand with me.”

Elias’ breath fogged in the cold water. His eyes flickered to the glowing sigil, then back to Kaelan’s face.

The ruins trembled.

The path of light surged toward the heart of the Spire.

And somewhere, deep in the Dominion Core, something answered.

Chapter 11

The courier reached Chancellor Dravik Solmarn in the small hours, when the bioluminescent algae in the Spire’s upper chambers bled into violet and the distant hum of the algae grid faded into near-silence.

Dravik woke without stirring, eyes opening in the dark like a predator’s. His private chamber was a curved alcove in the High Tower, its walls lined with pressure-glass panels that showed the city’s pulse—flickering veins of light pulsing through the Dominion’s veins like a living thing. He didn’t need a clock. He felt the shifts in the water, the rhythm of the current, the breath of the abyss.

The courier knocked once at the outer door. Three seconds later, again. Dravik knew the cadence. Protocol. No one broke protocol.

“Enter,” he said.

The door dilated. A guard stood aside. The courier—a junior scrivener from the lower archives—stepped forward on webbed feet, arms extended. In her hands, a sealed vial of black glass, embossed with the Solmarn crest.

Dravik didn’t move. He gestured with two fingers.

She crossed the chamber slowly, carefully. Water rippled around her ankles. When she knelt, her gloved hands trembled.

He took the vial. The glass was cold, heavier than it looked. Inside, a single drop of blood—thick, dark, iridescent under the low light.

Royal blood.

Not just any blood.

*Maris* blood.

Dravik’s fingers tightened. His pulse didn’t change. His breathing didn’t alter. But beneath the surface, the algae in his personal chamber darkened for the space of a breath. A reflex. Not an emotion. Never an emotion.

He set the vial on the obsidian desk.

“Who sent this?”

“The Vault Archives,” the courier said, voice steady but eyes darting. “Records Clerk Seris Veyra. She claims it was delivered anonymously to her inkwell this morning. She requests immediate verification.”

Dravik exhaled through his nose. Of course she did.

“You examined it?”

“Only visually. She said it was drawn at birth. From the infant’s cord.” The courier paused. “She called it proof.”

Dravik looked at the vial. Then at the Spire’s pulse on the wall. The algae flickered again—unsteady. A ripple of unease through the grid.

He stood.

“Wake the Heads of Council. All of them. Now.”

The courier rose. “Chancellor—”

“Now.”

She fled.

Dravik turned to the chamber’s main screen. The Spire’s status lit up in sharp lines: green, green, green—stable. But deeper in the system, beneath the visible layers, the algae grid showed a tremor. A pulse. Like a heartbeat in the dark.

He pulled on his regalia—a cloak of woven kelp-fiber, threaded with silver filaments that carried encrypted orders. His insignia glowed faintly as he fastened it to his chest.

Then he went to the Vault.
