The Abyssal Vault was a living thing. A spiral of coral and reinforced crystal, buried in the bedrock beneath the Spire’s roots. It held not only the Dominion’s archives, but the genetic memory of the bloodline—the proof of every ruler since the first Maris surfaced from the trench.

Dravik arrived to find the outer gate open. Not breached. Not forced. But open.

And the inner sanctum in chaos.

Guards stood in a tight knot, their tridents crossed like spears. Between them, a woman in wet robes, shaking. Nereia Solmarn. His niece.

Her hair floated in tangled spirals. Her face was pale, her lips parted. She clutched a datascroll with such force her knuckles bleached white.

Dravik dismissed the guards with a wave. They scattered like disturbed fish.

Nereia looked at him. Her eyes were wet.

“Uncle,” she said.

He didn’t answer. He walked past her, into the vault’s heart.

The central archive was a sphere of black coral, carved with the sigil of House Maris. It pulsed faintly, like a sleeping heart. But now, it flickered—alive, not dead.

He placed his hand on the coral.

It didn’t respond.

He turned to Nereia.

“What happened?”

She swallowed.

“I went to the Grand Atrium,” she said. “To pray. To think. The chandelier was still lit. The marriage banners… still there.” She gestured vaguely. “I needed to see it. To understand.”

Dravik said nothing.

“There was a man there,” she said. “Standing beneath the light. His sigil… it was glowing. Not like ours. Like fire.” She touched her own wrist—her Solmarn mark, dark and dormant. “He saw me. He smiled.”

Dravik’s jaw tightened.

“He called me *Nereia*.” She whispered the name like a curse. “He knew who I was. But he wasn’t afraid. He wasn’t hiding.” She looked at Dravik, desperate. “He *wanted* me to see.”

Dravik stepped closer.

“Describe him.”

“Tall. Pale. Scars on his wrists.” She hesitated. “His eyes… they were the same color as the chandelier when it flickers—almost silver. Not Maris green. Not Solmarn blue. *Different.*”

Dravik went still.

“And his sigil,” Nereia said, voice breaking. “It wasn’t just glowing. It was *mapping*. Like veins through his skin. Like the throne in the Rise.” She pulled her sleeve up, revealing the dormant Solmarn mark. “He had the full Maris sigil. Down his arm. Across his chest.”

Dravik exhaled slowly.

“He told me his name,” Nereia said. “Before I ran.”

Dravik waited.

“Kaelan,” she said. “Kaelan Veyne.”

The name hung in the water like a blade.

Dravik’s fingers curled into fists.

Then he looked at the vial on his belt—the royal blood he’d held moments ago.

He pulled it out.

“Where did you find this?” he asked quietly.

Nereia didn’t understand.

“In the Atrium,” she said. “He gave it to me. Said it was proof. Said I should give it to you. That you’d understand when you saw it.”

Dravik stared at the vial.

Then at Nereia’s tear-streaked face.

Then he understood.

Too late.

Kaelan Veyne hadn’t come to destroy the throne.

He’d come to *wear* it.

And he’d used Nereia to deliver the final proof.

Dravik turned to the central archive. The coral pulsed again—faster, louder.

The guards behind him shifted.

Dravik’s voice was steel.

“Seal the Spire,” he said. “No one in. No one out.”

Nereia’s breath hitched.

He looked at her.

“You,” he said. “You will come with me.”

She didn’t resist as he gripped her arm. The water around them darkened—the algae grid reacting to his command.

The Spire began to lock down.

And far above, in the Grand Atrium, the chandelier of captive light pulsed once—bright as a star.

Then it dimmed.

Not failing.

Yielding.

Chapter 12

The Grand Atrium had not changed.

Kaelan knew this place. He had walked its mosaic floors as Seris’s scholar, as the quiet man with ink-stained fingers and a manuscript to defend. He had stood beneath this chandelier before—its captive light trapped in pressure-glass, pulsing like a slow heartbeat through the Spire’s veins. He had even written a paper on the architecture: how the glass mimicked the bioluminescent bloom of deep-sea anemones, how the council had bent nature to their will.

But today, the light bent to him.

His sigil burned across his chest, searing through skin and memory. A crown of living flame trapped beneath his flesh. It had never glowed like this—not when he bled for Seris in the archives, not when he touched the Maris throne in the ruins of the Rise. Now it flared in full radiance, veins of silver and gold threading his arms, his neck, his face. The algae in the surrounding water rose with him, lifting like a crown of kelp in slow motion, tendrils curling toward the chandelier.

The crowd of councilors and guests recoiled. Some dropped to their knees. Others stumbled back, hands pressing against the glass barriers that separated them from the Atrium’s open water. The hum of the algae grid—usually a constant drone—faltered. Something ancient and sleeping had awoken.

Dravik Solmarn entered through the eastern archway, flanked by four armored guards. His cloak of kelp-fiber rippled behind him, silver filaments flashing like warning lights. His face was unreadable, as ever. But the algae in his personal chambers had darkened when he saw the vial. He had felt the pulse.

He stopped.

His eyes locked onto Kaelan.

Not with recognition.

With *refusal*.

“This is impossible,” Dravik said.

His voice carried across the Atrium. The water carried it farther. A ripple of sound, a disturbance in the current. Kaelan did not answer. He did not need to.

The chandelier flickered.

Not a malfunction. Not a surge.

A response.

Dravik’s guards shifted, their tridents lowered. One raised a hand toward the comm panel on his belt—likely to order containment, lockdown, silence. Dravik’s glare stopped him.

“Prince Kaelan Veyne,” Dravik said. The name tasted like poison. “Or should I say… *Heir*?”

Kaelan smiled. It was not triumph. It was acknowledgment.

A woman stepped forward from the crowd. Nereia Solmarn. Her wedding gown—white silk woven with silver thread—drifted around her like ghostly petals. Her face was pale, her lips parted. She clutched the vial of black glass, the royal blood, in one trembling hand.

She did not kneel.

She did not bow.

She looked at Kaelan as if he had just pulled the sky down to the sea floor.

“You gave me this,” she said.

“Yes,” Kaelan replied.

“Why?”

He met her gaze. “Because you are the last Solmarn who still has a choice.”

Her breath hitched. The algae in the water around her pulsed—reflexive, autonomic. Like the grid itself was listening.

Dravik stepped forward. The guards did not follow. They stood frozen, as if the Atrium’s gravity had shifted.

“You are not Maris,” Dravik said. “You are a lie. A ghost written in ink and blood.”

Kaelan raised his hand. The sigil flared brighter. Light bled into the water, casting long shadows across the mosaic floor. The tiles depicted the history of the Dominion—rising civilizations, the first surfacing of the Maris dynasty, the founding of the Spire. But in the center, beneath the chandelier, was a blank space. A wound in the story.

A place reserved for the heir.

Dravik’s jaw tightened.

“Your manuscript,” Dravik said suddenly. “The one you published under Seris Veyra’s name. The one that plagiarized the royal cipher.”

Kaelan did not flinch.

“Yes.”

“It contained the cipher,” Dravik continued. “The one that activates the royal archives. The one that only a Maris heir could know.”

Kaelan inclined his head.

“And the footnotes,” Dravik said. “The ones no scholar would notice. The ones that traced back to *me*.”

A murmur rippled through the councilors. Some paled. Others gripped their risers. One—a woman with silver-threaded hair—clutched her chest.

Kaelan exhaled slowly.

“You always suspected,” he said. “You just never looked.”

The algae in the chandelier dimmed.

Not failing.

Yielding.

Dravik’s hand went to his sword. Not to draw it. To steady himself.

“You are not the prince,” Dravik said. “You are a fraud. A thief of lineage. A—”

“Enough.”

The voice cut through the water like a blade.

Captain Elias Torin stepped from the western archway, wet armor glistening, trident held low but ready. His eyes were fixed on Kaelan—not with loyalty, not with duty, but with something raw and unreadable.

Dravik turned.

“Captain, arrest him.”

Elias did not move.

“On what charge?”

“Treason,” Dravik said. “Sorcery. Impersonation of royalty. Theft of Dominion secrets.”

Elias exhaled through his nose. Water rippled around his shoulders.

“There are no charges,” Elias said. “Not ones that hold water.”

The Atrium fell silent.

Kaelan watched Elias. The man who had once arrested him in the archives. The man who had stood in the Rise with a gun to his chest and called him a liar. The man who had seen the sigil bleed and said nothing.

Elias met Kaelan’s gaze.

Something passed between them. Not trust. Not yet. But recognition. Of a kind.

Dravik’s voice sliced through the moment.

“Captain, this is an order.”

Elias did not look away from Kaelan.

Then he lowered his trident.

“No,” he said.

The word hung in the water.

Dravik’s face darkened.

“You dare defy me?”

Elias met his gaze.

“I dare uphold the law,” Elias said. “The law states that only a Maris heir can wield the royal sigil. Only a Maris heir can command the algae grid. Only a Maris heir can—”

He gestured to the chandelier.

“—hold the throne.”

The algae in the Atrium surged upward, forming a crown around Kaelan’s head. The light bent toward him. The chandelier dimmed again—not from failure, but from surrender.

Dravik’s sword clattered to the floor.

The sound echoed through the Spire.

Then the guards moved.

Not to attack.

To seize.

Dravik did not resist as two guards clamped iron manacles around his wrists. The algae in his cloak dimmed. The man was a shell now. A monument crumbling.

Kaelan watched as Dravik was dragged away. No defiance. No curse. No final words.

Only silence.

The Atrium remained still. The councilors stood frozen, their breathing shallow. The algae in the water pulsed once—slow, steady, like a heartbeat.

Nereia stepped forward. She set the vial of royal blood on the mosaic floor between them. It rolled slightly, caught in the current, before settling.

Then she knelt.

Not as a bride.

As a subject.

Kaelan looked down at her.

“You were never the enemy,” he said.

She didn’t answer.

The chandelier dimmed again.

And for the first time in fifteen years, the light in the Grand Atrium belonged to a Maris heir.

Not as a ghost.

But as a king.

Chapter 13

The Mirror Chamber was a disk of black basalt suspended in the heart of the Grand Atrium. It had once been a throne—before the Maris dynasty fell, before the Solmarns rose. The surface was rippled glass, polished to reflect not faces, but the soul. The Council used it for judgment. Dravik had used it to terrify. Kaelan had never stood before it.

Until now.

The algae in the water around him had dimmed to a funeral glow. The chandelier’s captive light no longer bent toward him. The Atrium felt hollow. The councilors were silent. Nereia still knelt on the mosaic floor, her wedding gown pooling around her like spilled ink. Dravik was gone, dragged away in chains, his name already fading from the ledgers.

Kaelan placed his palm against the black glass.

No reflection stared back. Only darkness.

Then—

A slow bloom of light.

The algae in his sigil flared. Not silver. Not gold. Black. The color of deep water, of absence, of truth. The Mirror drank it in. The reflection formed not from light, but from memory.

He saw himself.

Not the scholar with ink-stained fingers. Not the concubine’s shadow. Not the ghost of a dead dynasty.

He saw the infant cast into the abyss fifteen years ago. Saw the hands that pulled him from the dark—Seris’s father, the marine biologist, his face gaunt with grief and purpose. Saw the cottage in the cold trench, the shelves of jars filled with pulsing light, the nights spent listening to the currents whisper stories of a world above.

The reflection changed.

He saw Dravik, standing over a cradle, a vial of dark liquid in one hand, a rusted blade in the other. The infant’s cry was cut short. The ocean swallowed it whole. Then—

A door opening. A hand reaching in. A child pulled out, gasping, alive.

The Mirror showed him the truth. Not just his own. The truth of the Dominion.

The algae grid was dying.

Not from sabotage. Not from age.

From silence.

Kaelan’s breath fogged the glass. The reflection wavered. He wiped it away with his sleeve.

Nereia stood. The white silk of her gown was torn at the shoulder. A councilor offered her a blanket; she didn’t take it.

“You knew,” she said.

Kaelan didn’t answer.

“You gave me the vial. The blood. You let me believe I was harming you. Why?”

The Mirror’s surface rippled. The image of the infant returned.

“Because you are the last Solmarn who still has a choice,” he said.

Nereia’s fingers tightened around her wrist. A servant’s bracelet, plain silver. Not a bride’s. Not a heir’s.

“Choice in what?”

Kaelan turned from the Mirror. The basalt felt cold beneath his skin.

“In whether to fight the tide or ride it,” he said.

Nereia followed him as he walked toward the eastern archway, away from the dying light, toward the deeper dark of the Spire’s lower corridors.

“You’re not just reclaiming a throne,” she said. “You’re undoing fifteen years of peace.”

Kaelan stopped. A school of luminous fish scattered around them, their scales flashing like distress signals.

“Peace?” he said. “Or control?”

Nereia didn’t flinch. “You sound like Dravik.”

“No,” Kaelan said. “I sound like the ocean.”

The corridor ahead sloped downward, toward the Abyssal Vault. The air grew thicker. The algae here didn’t pulse. It barely breathed.

Seris was waiting at the Vault’s entrance.

She stood in the flickering half-light of a single bioluminescent sconce. Her scribe’s robes were rumpled, ink stains on her sleeves. Her face was pale, her eyes bright with something Kaelan didn’t recognize.

Fear.

Relief.

Guilt.

She held the inkwell in both hands. The vial of royal blood glinted inside it, black as the Mirror’s surface.

Kaelan stepped toward her.

Seris didn’t move.

“You published my manuscript,” she said.

“Yes.”

“You let the world think I was the thief.”

“Yes.”

“You let Dravik call you a fraud.”

Kaelan stopped a body’s length away. The inkwell trembled in her grip.

“And you let him call me your brother,” she said.

The words hung between them.

Kaelan reached out.

Seris flinched.

He didn’t take the inkwell. Instead, he lifted his hand and pressed his palm against the Vault’s door. The algae sigil in his skin flared again—black, then silver, then gold. The door’s lock hissed open.

Seris’s breath hitched.

“You’re not here to fight,” she said.

“No.”

“You’re here to remember.”

Kaelan didn’t answer. He pushed the door open.

The Vault yawned before them—a cavern of stone and silence, lined with shelves of glowing algae, scrolls of kelp-fiber, tablets of compressed light. At the far end stood the Maris archives: a pedestal of black coral, a single scroll curled around it like a sleeping serpent.

The royal cipher waited there.

Kaelan stepped inside. The Vault’s algae dimmed in response, as if sensing his presence.

Seris followed.

Nereia hesitated at the threshold. Then she crossed it, her gown whispering against the stone.

The inkwell’s glow pulsed in Seris’s hands.

“They’ll say you stole this,” she said. “They’ll say you forged it. They’ll say you’re a fraud.”

Kaelan walked toward the archives.

“Let them.”

Seris’s voice cracked.

“This blood—this vial—it’s all that’s left of the Maris line. You’ve been carrying it in your inkwell for weeks. Writing letters. Editing papers. Bleeding into footnotes. And I—”

She held the inkwell out.

“—I let them call you a thief.”

Kaelan turned.

Seris’s eyes were wet.

He took the inkwell.

The vial inside glowed.

Not with silver. Not with gold.

With black.

The color of truth.

The color of tide.

The color of a name no one had spoken in fifteen years.

Kaelan held the inkwell to his chest. The algae in his sigil flared one last time—black, then silver, then gold.

Then it went dark.

Seris stepped back.

Nereia knelt.

Kaelan looked down at the Vault’s floor. The mosaic there depicted a child, arms outstretched, falling into the abyss. Beneath it, in small, worn tiles, were words:

*I am the tide. I am the return.*

Kaelan knelt.

He set the inkwell on the floor between them.

Then he reached out and took Seris’s hand.

“Write a new decree,” he said.

Chapter 14

The Grand Council Chamber smelled of salt and old ambition.

Kaelan stood beneath the chandelier where once he had stood as a groom, now wearing the tattered robes of a scholar. The algae in the glass veins pulsed faintly, as if remembering fire. Fifty councilors watched from tiers of coral-stone. Chancellor Dravik’s empty chair gleamed like a broken tooth.

Elias Torin stood at the chamber’s mouth, his Dominion Guard armor polished but his knuckles white around the hilt of his trident. His eyes flickered to Kaelan’s sigil—dark, quiet now, waiting.

Kaelan spoke before the silence could harden.

“I do not come for your throne. I do not come for your algae. I come to end the silence.”

A councilor’s assistant dropped a stylus. It chimed against stone.

“You come for civil war,” replied Elder Veyla of the Eastern Trench. Her bioluminescent tattoos pulsed in slow warning. “The Dominion thrives on order. Chaos is not reform.”

Kaelan let his gaze slide past her to the algae grid map on the far wall—a living circuit of light, veins threading through cavern and trench. The heart of the Spire.

“Order without justice is tyranny,” he said. “Control without consent is theft. You call the algae yours. But it is the people’s. It has always been the people’s.”

Nereia Solmarn, still in her torn wedding gown, stood from the lower tier. A line of salt dried on her collarbone.

“You would dissolve the Council,” she said. “Break the grid. Hand power to every trench, every kelp farm, every bioluminescent farmer. Chaos.”

“Balance,” Kaelan said. “You call the algae yours because you control the valves. But the algae grows in the dark. It remembers the old dynasties. It remembers Maris.”

A ripple passed through the chamber. A councilor’s hand trembled over a ledger.

Elias Torin stepped forward. His voice cut the tension like a blade through kelp.

“The Trench of Echoes is forbidden,” he said. “No light. No sound. No return.”

Kaelan turned slowly. Their eyes met across the chamber.

“Then forbid it no longer.”

Elder Veyla scoffed. “You speak treason in the name of reform.”

Kaelan smiled. Not with warmth.

“Or reform in the name of life.”

Dravik’s empty seat creaked as someone leaned forward in shadow. The Acting Chancellor, Harkon Veyl, cleared his throat.

“You offer us nothing but disruption. The Night of the Eclipse approaches. The algae will darken. The people will panic. What will you do then?”

Kaelan unclasped the vial from his belt. The royal blood inside caught the dim light—a bead of deep black, suspended.

“When the algae darkens,” he said, “I will be the light.”

He placed the vial on the council table.

It did not roll. It did not spill.

It sat still.

A hush fell. The algae in the chandelier dimmed further, as if listening.

Harkon Veyl’s jaw tightened.

“You claim lineage through blood you kept secret. You claim authority through words stolen from your scribe. You claim power through a vial any charlatan could fill with ink.”

Kaelan did not flinch.

“Then take the vial,” he said. “Test it. Let the archives speak. The Mirror Chamber still holds the truth. I will stand there again. But not today.”

He turned to the chamber.

“You have two choices,” he said. “Dissolve the algae monopoly. Reform the Council. Share power with the people. Or resist. Cling to control. And face the Trench of Echoes—where bioluminescence dies forever.”

A junior councilor gasped. Elder Veyla’s tattoos flared violet in outrage.

Harkon Veyl stood, his chair scraping like a weapon unsheathed.

“You cannot dictate terms to the Dominion Council.”

“I just did.”

Elias Torin’s fingers flexed on his trident.

Kaelan ignored him. He walked to the chamber’s edge, where the first tier dipped toward the exit.

“Six weeks until the Eclipse,” he said. “Make your choice. But know this—if you choose control, I will open the Trench gates myself. And I will let the ocean decide what lives in the dark.”

He stepped into the corridor. The algae sconces flickered, confused, as if they did not know whether to obey the old order or the new.

Nereia followed him without a word. Her bare feet whispered against stone.

The Grand Atrium loomed ahead, vast and hollow, its once-great chandelier now a dim constellation of dying stars.

Kaelan paused beneath it.

Nereia stopped beside him. Her wedding veil was gone. Her hair tangled from windless halls.

“You’re not just asking for reform,” she said. “You’re asking for surrender.”

“No,” Kaelan said. “I’m asking for trust.”

She looked at him—really looked. Not at the scholar’s robe. Not at the fading sigil. At the man beneath.

“And the blood test?” she asked. “You could prove yourself in an hour.”

“Blood is memory,” Kaelan said. “But memory is not justice. The people do not need proof. They need choice.”

Nereia exhaled, slow and measured.

“You remind me of someone,” she said. “Someone who thought peace was more than silence.”

Kaelan did not answer.

They walked toward the Atrium’s eastern arch, where the deep corridors coiled into the Spire’s underbelly.

A guard stood at the archway—one of Elias’s men. He saluted Kaelan with a clenched fist.

Kaelan nodded. Then he turned to Nereia.

“Go home,” he said. “Wash your gown. Burn it if you must. But remember—you are the last Solmarn who still has a choice.”

Nereia’s lips parted. Before she could speak, Kaelan walked past the guard and into the corridor.

The light behind him dimmed.

Ahead, the corridor split—left to the Rise of Maris, right to the Abyssal Vault.

Kaelan turned left.

Nereia watched him go.

Then she turned right.

The guard hesitated only a moment before falling into step behind her.

Kaelan walked faster. The algae in the walls pulsed weakly, like a heartbeat fading. The air grew colder. The corridor narrowed.

He passed a mural of the old Maris king, his face erased by time, only the eyes remaining—gold, alive.

Kaelan stopped.

He pressed his palm to the mural.

Nothing happened.

No sigil flared. No door opened. The plaster stayed cold.

He exhaled.

A footstep echoed behind him.

Kaelan turned.

Elias Torin stood at the corridor’s end, bathed in the sickly glow of a dying sconce. His armor gleamed with old wear. His face was unreadable.

Kaelan said nothing.

Elias did not speak.

They stood in silence for three breaths.

Then Elias stepped forward.

He knelt not on one knee—for that would be a vow to a prince.

He knelt on two knees—for the city.

He placed his trident across the stone between them.

Then he looked up.

“I will be your sword,” he said. “And your shield… if you’ll have me.”

Chapter 15

The Rise of Maris had no throne.

Not anymore. The old dais stood broken, half-collapsed into the coral floor. Kaelan walked past it, his boots scuffing the smooth stone where once only priest-kings had trod. The air smelled of brine and old cedar—nobody had lit incense here in generations.

Seris knelt at the base of the incomplete throne platform, her scribe’s desk a plank balanced on two iron stands. A ledger lay open, the pages already lined with her neat script. At her feet, a small vial of black blood sat in a tray of crushed shell, catching the faint blue-green glow of the chandelier above.

Kaelan stopped beside her.

“You’re signing laws as if they’re final,” he said. “They’re not.”

Seris didn’t look up. She dipped her quill into the vial—ink mixed with royal blood—then pressed it to the page.

“I’m signing them as if they are,” she replied. “That’s the difference between a law and a suggestion.”

A councilor cleared his throat behind them. Elder Harkon Veyl stood in the arched doorway, his bioluminescent tattoos pulsing a slow, nervous amber.

“You cannot convene court without the Council’s sanction,” he said. “The Dominion does not recognize—”

“Recognize this.”

Kaelan stepped forward. He pressed two fingers to his left shoulder—the place where the Maris sigil slept beneath his skin. He had never let it show in public before. Not once.

Light bloomed.

Not a flare. Not a burst. A steady, deep pulse like a slow heartbeat, golden and quiet. The algae in the chandelier brightened in response, its veins throbbing in sync.

Harkon’s jaw tightened.

Seris didn’t even glance up. She signed the final line, then blew gently across the wet ink. The page dried instantly, the blood sealing the law into the parchment.

Kaelan exhaled.

The sigil dimmed—not faded, but settled, like a fire banked for the night.

Seris capped the vial and tucked it into her belt pouch.

“There,” she said. “Twelve laws. Twelve cracks in the dam.”

Harkon stepped closer. His shadow fell across the ledger.

“You claim authority through blood and ink,” he said. “But ink fades. Blood washes away.”

“Not this,” Kaelan said. “Not anymore.”

A gust of current rushed through the chamber, carrying the scent of kelp and deep water. The algae in the ceiling flickered, confused, as if unsure whether to light the room or drown it in darkness.

Seris closed the ledger with a snap.

“He claims the throne,” she said to Harkon. “Not by conquest. Not by decree. By work. By proof. You want order? Here it is.”

Harkon’s tattoos pulsed red in warning.

“You do not understand the cost of what you’ve started.”

“I do,” Seris said. “Which is why I’m staying.” She met Kaelan’s eyes. “Someone has to make sure the ink doesn’t run.”

Kaelan smiled. Small. Real.

“You always were better at endings than beginnings.”

She tossed him the ledger. He caught it one-handed.

Outside, the murmur of counselors rose—some in outrage, some in reluctant curiosity. The Rise had been a tomb for fifteen years. Now it breathed.

Kaelan turned to the chamber’s opening.

Through the archway, the Grand Atrium swam into view—a cavern of broken chandeliers and flickering algae veins, once the heart of the Dominion’s power. Now it pulsed with energy that was no longer stolen.

A figure stood beneath the largest broken fixture, back to them.

Nereia Solmarn wore a plain robe of undyed kelp-fiber, belted at the waist. Her hair was loose, still damp from the pools outside. She held a scroll in one hand, her fingers tight around the edges.

Kaelan walked toward her.

She didn’t turn.

“You signed,” she said. “You made it real.”

“Yes.”

“Without a coronation. Without witnesses. Without a crown.”

“Crowns are heavy,” Kaelan said. “I prefer light.”

Nereia finally turned. Her eyes were clear, bright in the dim algae glow.

“People will call you king,” she said. “Even if you never wear the word.”

“Let them.”

She held out the scroll.

“I wrote my abdication,” she said. “I renounce my name. My claim. My blood.”

Kaelan didn’t take it.

“You don’t have to.”

“I do.” She stepped closer. The salt on her skin caught the light. “The Council will still resist. The algae grid will still fight. But you—you are not Dravik. You don’t force. You invite.”

A flicker crossed Kaelan’s face. Not doubt. Resolve.

“And if they refuse the invitation?”

Nereia’s lips curved, nearly a smile.

“Then you open the Trench gates. Let the ocean decide. You already told them that.”

Kaelan took the scroll. The parchment was brittle with age, the ink still wet.

“I could have you arrested for treason,” he said.

“You could,” she said. “But you won’t.”

He unrolled it slowly. The words were simple: *I, Nereia Solmarn, lay down my claim to the Dominion Council. Let the people choose their path.*

No flourish. No plea. Just truth.

Kaelan rolled it again and tucked it into his belt.

Nereia breathed in, slow and deep.

“What now?”

“Now,” said a voice behind them, “we wait for the Eclipse.”

Captain Elias Torin stood in the archway, his Dominion Guard armor cleaned but scarred. He wore no trident now. His hands were empty. His knuckles were white.

Kaelan turned fully to face him.

Elias stepped forward. Not kneeling this time. Not bowing. Just walking into the light of a reborn throne room.

Nereia retreated to the ledger desk, giving them space. She didn’t look back.

Kaelan held Elias’s gaze.

“You swore an oath,” Kaelan said. “To the Council. To Dravik.”

Elias stopped three paces away.

“I swore an oath to the Dominion,” he said. “And the Dominion just changed.”

A silence settled, heavy and alive.

Elias’s fingers twitched at his side.

“You could have run,” Kaelan said. “After the Vault. After the Mirror. You could have arrested me in the corridor.”

“Could have,” Elias said. “But the algae remembers. And so do I.”

Kaelan reached up and pulled the royal cipher from beneath his robe—the one he had kept hidden for years. He held it out.

Elias didn’t take it.

“No,” Kaelan said. “Keep it. It’s yours now.”

Elias stared at the cipher—a bent scrap of shell etched with the old Maris sigil.

“What does it do?”

“Opens the Vault,” Kaelan said. “But more than that—it opens choice. It lets the people decide what lives in the light.”

Elias exhaled through his nose.

“You’re asking me to lead the Guard against my own chain of command.”

“No,” Kaelan said. “I’m asking you to lead it with me.”

Elias’s eyes darkened.

“You don’t trust the Council. You don’t trust the algae. You don’t even trust your own sigil to stay bright.”

Kaelan didn’t look away.

“Trust isn’t the point. Action is.”

Elias stepped closer. Close enough that Kaelan smelled the iron in his armor, the salt on his skin, the faintest trace of kelp from his cloak.

“And if the Eclipse comes and the algae dies?” Elias asked. “What then?”

Kaelan met his gaze.

“Then we swim in the dark,” he said. “Together.”

Elias held his breath.

Outside, the algae in the Rise’s ceiling pulsed in rhythm—one bright, one dim, one bright.

Like a heart.

Elias reached out. Not for the cipher. For Kaelan’s wrist.

He turned it gently.

The sigil glowed—not bright, not dim. Steady. Calm.

Like a heart at peace.

Kaelan didn’t pull away.

Elias let go.

He walked past, toward the ledger where Seris watched, unblinking. He bowed his head to her.

“Scribe,” he said. “Record the laws. Record the names. Record the truth.”

Seris nodded once.

Elias turned back to Kaelan.

“Six weeks,” he said. “The Eclipse. The people will panic. The Council will fracture. The algae grid will shudder.”

Kaelan folded his arms.

“And?”

Elias’s voice dropped.

“And I command the Guard. I will keep the peace. But if blood is spilled… if the people turn on each other… if the grid fails and the city starves in the dark…” He met Kaelan’s eyes. “I will hold you responsible.”

Kaelan smiled. Not with warmth. With truth.

“Good.”

Elias gave a single nod. Then he walked out, his boots ringing against the coral stone.

Nereia rose from the desk.

“He’ll do it,” she said. “He’ll keep order. But he’ll blame you if it breaks.”

Kaelan watched the archway where Elias had disappeared.

“Let him blame me,” he said. “Blame is better than silence.”

Seris closed the ledger with a soft thud.

“What do we do now?” she asked.

Kaelan looked at the throne platform, still incomplete. Still waiting.

“Now,” he said, “we wait for the ocean to decide what it wants to be.”
